title: NIS2 Cryptography Policy Compliance — TLS and Certificate Posture
exclusions: Does not operate hardware security modules, manage root key ceremonies, or decide which national algorithms are legally recognized—only organizational policy alignment and monitoring.
evidence: `UC-22.2.29` correlation workbook, redacted `stream:tls` pcap-to-SPL reconciliation notes, inventory CSV hashes, and RFC3161 signature bundle stored per `evidenceSigning` alongside PKI CAB drafts.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Art.21(2)(h) requires policies regarding cryptography and encryption. The UC monitors TLS certificate validity, cipher suite compliance, and certificate authority usage across the estate. It cannot confirm that the underlying cryptography policy addresses key rotation schedules or post-quantum readiness, hence partial.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIS2 Art.21(2)(h) (Cryptography and encryption) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.2.29: NIS2 Cryptography Policy Compliance — TLS and Certificate Posture.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.2.29' running on TLS metadata from Stream, certificate inventory, nis2_crypto_policy.csv (min_tls_version, allowed_ciphers), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Staging VIP intentionally negotiates TLSv1.0 on port 443 while scoped map marks `nis2_in_scope=true`; within one hour the alert surfaces `weak_tls=1`, `service_name=payments_api`, proving enforcement targets in-scope listeners only.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Production uplift completes with TLSv1.3 only, new certificates carry ninety-day lifetimes managed via automated ACME, scoped map retires the lab IP, and seven consecutive runs return zero rows while external Qualys SSL scan shows grade A for the same endpoint.

dataSources: `index=pki` `sourcetype=stream:tls` (dest_ip, dest_port, tls_version, cipher, expiry_epoch) via Splunk Add-on for Stream ([Splunkbase 1809](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/1809)); `index=certificates` `sourcetype=certificate:inventory` for `ssl_serial`, `ssl_issuer`, `ssl_end_time` aligned to CIM Certificates; lookup `nis2_crypto_policy.csv` supplying `min_tls_version`, `allowed_ciphers`; optional Splunk Enterprise Security ([Splunkbase 263](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/263)) for service_name enrichment.
app: Splunk Add-on for Stream (Splunkbase 1809), Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (Splunkbase 1621)
spl:
index=pki sourcetype="stream:tls" earliest=-24h
| stats latest(tls_version) as tls_version latest(cipher) as cipher values(expiry_epoch) as cert_expiry by dest_ip dest_port
| lookup nis2_scoped_service_map dest_ip OUTPUT service_name nis2_in_scope
| where nis2_in_scope="true"
| eval weak_tls=if(match(tls_version,"SSLv|TLSv1\.0|TLSv1\.1"),1,0)
| eval cert_problem=if(cert_expiry < relative_time(now(),"+30d@d"),1,0)
| where weak_tls=1 OR cert_problem=1
| table service_name, dest_ip, dest_port, tls_version, cipher, cert_problem
description: Correlates passive TLS observations from Stream with inventory-derived certificate expirations and `nis2_scoped_service_map` to list only NIS2-scoped listeners that run outdated protocols or face thirty-day renewals.
value: Weak TLS versions are invisible until a pen test or outage; combining wire-derived protocol data with scoped service context lets risk owners prioritize renewals that actually map to regulated workloads instead of every lab VM in the enterprise.
implementation: (1) Mirror production VLANs to Stream. (2) Maintain `nis2_scoped_service_map`. (3) Import `nis2_crypto_policy.csv`. (4) Alert on `weak_tls=1`. (5) PKI workflow webhook on `cert_problem=1`. (6) Annual evidence export + `evidenceSigning`.
visualization: Table with color rules on tls_version, timeline for cert_expiry, map of dest_ip.
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t min(All_Certificates.ssl_end_time) as soonest from datamodel=Certificates.All_Certificates by All_Certificates.ssl_subject | where soonest < relative_time(now(),"+30d")
dataModelAcceleration: Accelerate Certificates if inventory model used heavily
knownFalsePositives: 1) **Legacy mainframe TN3270 TLS gateways** cannot upgrade beyond TLSv1.1 until vendor patch. *Distinguish:* `service_name` matches `nis2_crypto_policy.csv` exception list. *Suppress:* `lookup nis2_crypto_policy service_name OUTPUT waive_until`. 2) **Stream misses TLS 1.3 downgrade to plaintext** because capture ACL changed. *Distinguish:* event volume drop >50%. *Suppress:* do not suppress—open infra ticket; only pause alert via time-bound exception register. 3) **Shared load balancer IP reused** mapping wrong `service_name`. *Distinguish:* `dest_port` differing entries. *Suppress:* enrich via `lookup vip_service dest_ip dest_port`. 4) **Certificate inventory stale** triggers false cert_problem. *Distinguish:* `certificate:inventory _indextime` older than 7d. *Suppress:* join latest inventory `| join ...` with freshness filter. 5) **Pentest TLS downgrade tooling** during sanctioned test week. *Distinguish:* `src_ip` in `pentest_supernet`. *Suppress:* `where NOT cidrmatch("192.0.2.0/24",src_ip)` assuming Stream forwards client IP.
equipmentModels: Qualys SSL Labs / Tenable.io (external TLS posture validation)
Splunk Add-on for Stream (Splunkbase 1809, stream:tls field extractions)
Splunk Enterprise Security 7.x (asset enrichment, service scoping)
Splunk Stream Independent Forwarder (passive TLS handshake capture)
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Indexes**: `pki` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Lookups**: `nis2_scoped_service_map` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('nis2_scoped_service_map')`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `Art.21(2)(h)`. The control objective is: "Evidence that NIS2 Art.21(2)(h) (Cryptography and encryption) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.2.29: NIS2 Cryptography Policy Compliance — TLS and Certificate Posture." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into `pki` under `stream:tls`, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


6 operational considerations apply to the rollout of this detection:


• **Hint 1:** Mirror production VLANs to Stream. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 2:** Maintain `nis2_scoped_service_map`. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 3:** Import `nis2_crypto_policy.csv`. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** Alert on `weak_tls=1`. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 5:** PKI workflow webhook on `cert_problem=1`. Routing targets (SOC, compliance team, specific queue) must be documented with named on-call owners; an 'alerts go to the team' runbook fails at 3am on Sunday.

• **Hint 6:** Annual evidence export + `evidenceSigning`. Report-generation cadence must match the regulatory reporting cycle; a monthly digest satisfies an annual review requirement but not a quarterly one.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "NIS2 Cryptography Policy Compliance — TLS and Certificate Posture," the aggregation produces the result shape required by the NIS2 control — each row is a summary record the regulation expects to be presentable at audit time.

This search enriches raw events with context from the lookup `nis2_scoped_service_map` — the compliance scope definition boundary register. The raw event stream alone cannot answer the compliance question because it does not carry the authoritative business attributes (`service_name nis2_in_scope`) — these live in a register maintained outside the monitored system. The `| lookup` joins at search time on `dest_ip`, making the search results only as accurate as the lookup's freshness.

Lookup ownership and freshness: This is the single most common failure mode for lookup-based compliance searches. The `nis2_scoped_service_map` table must be owned by a named team with a documented refresh cadence and staleness alerting. A minimum operational SLA is: (1) lookup refreshed at a cadence matching the risk domain (daily for access control, weekly for inventory, quarterly for vendor registers), (2) automated alert when the lookup has not been rewritten in 2x the refresh interval, (3) a row-count sanity check (alert if row count changes by more than 20% between refreshes — catches truncation bugs in upstream exports).

Case sensitivity: Splunk's lookup matching is case-sensitive by default. If the raw event field and the lookup key differ in case (e.g. `Account_Name=JSMITH` vs lookup `samaccountname=jsmith`), the join silently returns null. Normalize both sides with `| eval dest_ip = lower(dest_ip)` before the lookup, or set `case_sensitive_match = false` in the lookup's `transforms.conf` stanza. Search across the last 24 hours.

**CIM / accelerated variant:** The detection also has an accelerated variant using `| tstats` against the CIM `Certificates` datamodel:

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t min(All_Certificates.ssl_end_time) as soonest from datamodel=Certificates.All_Certificates by All_Certificates.ssl_subject | where soonest < relative_time(now(),"+30d")
```

When to prefer this variant: once the underlying datamodel is accelerated (verify in Settings > Data Models > `Certificates` > status=100%), this query runs 10-50x faster than the raw variant and can feasibly cover multi-month windows for audit reporting. When to avoid: if your field extractions are app-specific and the CIM tagging is incomplete, the `tstats` variant will silently under-count. Always compare raw vs accelerated counts on a known day before swapping the production schedule. Maintain both variants in the UC — the raw is the primary for accuracy, the accelerated is the performance optimisation for dashboarding.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.2.29: NIS2 Cryptography Policy Compliance — TLS and Certificate Po]
cron_schedule = 0 1 * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -24h
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.2.29,reg=NIS2
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=pki sourcetype=stream:tls earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `cert_expiry`, `cert_problem`, `cipher`, `dest_ip`, `dest_port`, `service_name`, `tls_version`, `weak_tls`. Run:

```spl
index=pki sourcetype=stream:tls earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table cert_expiry, cert_problem, cipher, dest_ip, dest_port, service_name, tls_version, weak_tls
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(c) Lookup freshness:** The detection joins against `nis2_scoped_service_map`. Check its modification time and row count:

```spl
| inputlookup nis2_scoped_service_map
| eval _lookup_row=1
| stats count as rows
```

Compare the row count to the expected baseline; an unexpected drop is a sign the upstream export truncated.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.2.29: NIS2 evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Table With Color Rules On Tls Version (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 2 — Timeline For Cert Expiry (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 3 — Map Of Dest Ip (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**No results when violations exist:** Check the lookup's case sensitivity (Splunk lookups are case-sensitive by default) and null handling — a null on the input side produces a silent miss, not an error.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**NIS2-specific failure modes:**

• 24/7 reporting obligation (Art. 23): NIS2 requires early warning within 24 hours of awareness of a significant incident. Detections that back this SLA must compute elapsed time from the SOC's first-acknowledged timestamp (not the resolve-time) and must trigger independently of business hours — the regulatory clock does not pause overnight.

• Member-state variation: NIS2 is transposed individually by each EU Member State with some latitude on thresholds and sector scoping. A detection correct for German IT-SiG 3 (BSI) may be misaligned with Dutch CWV or French LPM. Use `nis2_member_state_config.csv` to parametrize thresholds per jurisdiction; never hardcode.

• Essential vs Important entity criteria: the applicable obligations depend on sector + size. Before alerting, verify the monitored entity's designation; a detection firing on an out-of-scope subsidiary causes unnecessary regulatory notifications that erode trust with the national CSIRT.

• Supply-chain (Art. 21(2)(d)) extent: NIS2 explicitly includes direct suppliers' cybersecurity posture. Detections must cover monitored-by-you-not-owned-by-you infrastructure (outsourced SaaS, managed SIEM tenants) — these are in scope even if operationally run by a third party. BA/BAA-style governance applies.
grandmaExplanation: We listen quietly to how computers greet each other on the network and check whether they use strong secret handshakes. If a handshake is too old or a security badge is about to run out of date, we flag only the systems the law cares about most.
